name: Starting a new program in January
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user is joining Mars College, a 3-month off-grid desert program running January-March 2026 at Bombay Beach near the Salton Sea, focused on AI, art, tech, and self-sufficient living. They have 25 years of metal fabrication experience and are currently working on dual-LLM orchestration systems. Their Sun Valley workspace lease expires mid-January 2026, creating a timing overlap with the Mars College program. The conversation began with the user sharing a transcript from an all-hands meeting about the campus layout and AI curriculum plans, including discussions of "Chiba" (the main building structure) and a "living building" concept that would integrate AI systems throughout the physical infrastructure.

Claude helped the user understand the program structure and created two major artifacts. First was a comprehensive decision timeline mapping out arrival options (early December build crew vs standard January arrival vs hybrid approach), workspace considerations (securing space now vs storage vs full commitment to Mars), and integration opportunities where the user's fabrication and AI skills could converge. The user then requested a visual campus map, leading Claude to create an interactive 2D layout showing Chiba's three-bay structure, kitchen placement, AI camp building, projection areas, and residential zones with ground floor and upper floor views.

The conversation evolved toward designing a modular offline AI infrastructure system for the campus. Initially considering the "living building" project as a potential thesis, the user clarified this would be a side contribution while pursuing a different main project. They showed interest in an offline AI assistant concept running on Raspberry Pi 5 with Whisper, Ollama, and Piper, but expanded this into a modular framework where the offline AI serves as a base platform that workshop participants could extend throughout the semester. Claude created a detailed technical infrastructure schematic showing power distribution, data networks, terminal locations, sensor placement, and intercom systems across the campus.

When the user specified a $350 hardware budget constraint, Claude redesigned the system from 6 terminals down to 2-3 units and created comprehensive specifications for a fabricable terminal design. The final artifact detailed a 300mm × 400mm × 80mm terminal unit using ESP32-S3 controllers, 2.8" TFT displays, I2S MEMS microphones, and 5W speakers, with complete assembly instructions and a bill of materials hitting exactly the $350 budget target. The design emphasizes the user's metal fabrication expertise with sheet metal enclosures, mounting systems, and desert-hardened construction suitable for the off-grid environment.

chat_messages.text: I just got out of an all hands camp meeting for this program im joining in january:

attachments.extracted_content:
Mars College
Mars College


Announcing Mars College 2026
An open call to participate in our high-tech, off-grid, pop-up college
Mars College
Aug 09, 2025




Mars College is coming back for another semester in the desert, and we’d love for you to join us.

Apply to Mars College 2026

Mars College is an annual 3-month program dedicated to helping people adopt a low-cost, high-tech, self-sufficient life. We are equal parts lifestyle incubator, educational program, and intentional community. Mars College is located next to the gorgeous Salton Sea in Southern California.

In the six years we’ve been doing this, many Martian alumni have called it the best experience of their lives.

Here’s how it works:

Phase 1: Build (Nov-Dec 2025)
Before most Martians arrive, a small crew builds an off-grid village in the open desert a mile outside the town of Bombay Beach, and equips it with water, solar power, and high-speed internet.






Builds from years past
Phase 2: Learn (Jan-Mar 2026)
Most Martians arrive on or around New Years. The first two weeks are dedicated to post-build decoration and outfitting our campus for life. The second week is also Ideas Week, a week of orientation, presentations, and getting to know each other.




Lightning talks and dinners during Ideas Week
The first half of the semester is an unconference-style academic program. There is an open calendar for Martians to offer workshops sharing their skills and passions. There are a number of academic tracks and topics including (but not limited to): Creative AI, bodywork, vibecoding, off-grid building, permaculture, creative writing, game development, music-making, science fiction and more. Every day is filled with classes, workshops and events created by Martians.










Living and learning in the desert
The second half of the semester is more loose in structure, and classes give way to collaborations and group project work in preparation for our semester-end final exhibition, Mars Electronica.









Mars Electronica includes performances, a gallery, a film festival, and final presentations from Martians
Phase 3: Unbuild (Apr 2026)
Mars College is dismantled in mid-April, and we leave the land as barren as it was when we got there, to wait until next year to do it all over again.




Left without a trace
Over the next few weeks, we will be sharing more info about our upcoming plans, on our Substack, Instagram, and Twitter.

FAQ
Why are you doing this?
Why not? Mars College is an experiment in what’s possible. We appear, build our campus, get to know one another, make friends, learn new things, make art, showcase our work, and disappear. Then we do it all again.

Where will I live? What’s it like to live there?
Living on Mars is a choose-your-own-adventure situation. Generally, Martians join a living co-op to meet their basic needs such as food and shelter. If you are a new Martian, this will be part of the interview process.

Martians living on Mars can build dorms or other shelter, camp, or live in a vehicle. The Mars campus will have several kitchens stewarded by the living co-ops, and water, internet, and solar power are provided as campus services.

Some Martians elect to get housing in Bombay Beach, which is also possible, for those who prefer a house. You can make your preferences clear in the application.

How much does it cost?
This will depend on how you choose to structure your living situation while on Mars. For the three-month program, expect Mars College dues to be somewhere around $500-1,000 total. This is used to cover the cost of high-speed internet, portapotties, water, and building materials. This notably does not include your food/grocery costs, which are generally arranged separately.

Sounds great, how do I apply?
Fill out the application form here. We will start having interviews and rolling admissions in late August/early September.

Apply to Mars College 2026

Substack is the home for great culture

For the past few years, we’ve organized a creative AI study at Mars College. It has included classes, workshops, and group activities aimed at helping people learn how to use AI tools for their own art, research, and personal projects. This year marks the fourth year of the program and we’re more excited about it than ever.

Apply to Mars College

Wait, what is Mars College? Read here.

Martian Academics
Mars College isn’t a webinar or virtual class. It’s a solar-powered off-grid campus built from the ground up each winter in a dry lakebed in the California desert. Martians assemble their own living spaces, kitchens and labs, then roll a curriculum that merges hands‑on homesteading with self‑guided research. At the end of the semester, campus is disassembled and disbanded, to reconvene at the same time next year. This rugged lifestyle isn’t for everyone; it’s harsh, dusty, and tests your ingenuity and resilience. But it forges a tight-knit community of learners and refuge from the hamster wheel of urban professional life.




Digital architecture workshop by Andrew Heom, 2023
Mars College extends the self‑reliance ethos of desert communities to the technology domain; rather than fall for the nature vs technology dichotomy, we think AI is the secret superpower and enabler of the 21st century vagabond, enabling intrepid individuals to acquire new skills and knowledge quickly and cheaply, from even the most remote locations on Earth, no internet link required!

Academics at Mars are a mixture of a structured curriculum that aims to level up everyone’s literacy around contemporary AI tools, plus an unconference-style open calendar where participants propose and host their own sessions on diverse topics ranging from permaculture to creative writing to singing sea shanties.

AI at Mars College 2022-2025
The Creative AI major at Mars College was first formed in 2022 and focused on early image generation and chatbots.




Early AI experiments at Mars, via X.
As generative video and agents evolved, so too did our program. In the wake of the AI explosion of the last few years, the program grew in attendance and scope. Last year we began a serious study of AI agents, highlighted by several dozen experimental chatbots we placed in our private Discord server, each with a Martian creator learning on the fly how to make their agent represent their values.

Mars Research Curriculum
Mars College
·
October 20, 2024
Mars Research Curriculum
Mars College is gearing up for a new season, and one of the new camps this year is called Mars Research. Mars Research is dedicated to the study of AI, and this upcoming academic program will focus on generative AI. Let’s explore the tracks and courses that will shape the program.

Read full story
In early 2023, leading up to the Bombay Beach Biennale, despite the then-lack of commercially available AI video models, we collectively produced our first ever AI film festival. As AI video models rapidly progressed, the AI film academy formed and made the film festival a tradition. We are planning the 2026 film festival and will be guiding aspiring Martian creators learn how to produce the kinds of films that just a few years ago would have cost tens of millions of dollars to produce.


Past editions of AI program at Mars College
Mars Research
Mars Research
Mars College

Study Creative AI at Mars College
Study Creative AI at Mars College
Mars College

October 9, 2022
Read full story
The 2026 program
Announcing Mars College 2026
Mars College
·
Aug 9
Announcing Mars College 2026
Mars College is coming back for another semester in the desert, and we’d love for you to join us.

Read full story
Now going into its 5th edition, the creative AI program is expanding into new frontiers, including a planned game development track. Even as we delve into abstract and heady topics like world models, the purpose remains deeply practical. By studying how to build and manipulate such AIs, participants learn how to assemble personal systems that assist them in everything from learning home‑grown solar arrays to making money online to support their itinerant lifestyle.

Our aim is to help every participant build a personal AI: a bespoke agent that augments their learning, helps with creative projects and makes life on Mars more interesting. We give students free compute on Eden.art and teach them how to wield these models.

Over the coming weeks, we plan to announce more about the AI program. If you’re already psyched about the program, apply today! Otherwise, stay tuned for more details by subscribing here.

chat_messages.text:
Ahab just got out of a meeting for Mars College, which he's joining in January. Looking at the documents, Mars College is a 3-month off-grid desert program in Bombay Beach near the Salton Sea that runs Jan-Mar 2026. It's an educational/community experience focused on AI, art, tech, and self-sufficient living.

Given his preferences for direct, task-oriented communication and that he has ADD, I should:
1. Acknowledge what he's doing without fluff
2. Be ready to help with whatever comes next - he might want to debrief, plan, or work through something specific
3. Keep it focused and actionable

He mentioned it's an "all hands camp meeting" - so likely logistics, expectations, or planning discussions. I should ask what's on his mind or what he needs help with, but keep it concise and direct.Nice—Mars College looks like it's right in your wheelhouse. Creative AI, fabrication, off-grid building, game dev track... and it lines up with your workspace timeline since your Sun Valley lease is up mid-January anyway.

What's the current state? What are you thinking about or need to work through coming out of that meeting?

chat_messages.text:
The user is sharing a transcript from a Mars College all-hands meeting. Let me parse through this to understand what's being discussed:

Key points from the transcript:

Intro
0:00
Hello everyone, welcome back to the channel. Today we are building a next level AI gadget, 
0:06
a fully offline voice assistant that can  talk, listen, and show emotions on the mini display.
0:12
It runs on the Raspberry Pi 5B (Correction: Raspberry Pi 5)
0:16
with the Whislay Hat, and the PiSugar 3 Plus battery pack. 
0:21
This little buddy works anywhere without  internet. Private, portable, and playful.  
0:27
So let's get started to make your own AI friend.
Hardware setup
0:32
Here is our setup checklist.
0:38
For the Raspberry Pi 5B (Correction: Raspberry Pi 5), I recommend using the model with 8G of RAM or more,  
0:45
because running all the AI software together,  typically use around 4G of memory on its own.
0:52
This setup gets hot enough to cook breakfast,  
0:55
so an active cooler is a must if you want  stable performance and a happy raspberry pi.
1:01
Since we're installing the  PiSugar underneath the Pi, 
1:04
the cooler's push pin is a little too tall. We  
1:07
need to gently bending it outward  and press it to reduce the height.
1:18
Then add a stackable GPIO header so the  Whisplay Hat will fit perfectly on top.
1:25
Next, install the PiSugar battery by aligning  
1:28
its pins with the GPIO side and  securing it with the four screws.
1:36
Finally, place the Whisplay Hat on  top of the GPIO header and gently  
1:42
press down on both sides until  it’s fully seated on the pins.
1:47
And just like that, the  hardware assembly is complete.
Flash sd card
1:51
Now, let’s dive into the software part.
1:56
I’m using the Raspberry Pi Imager to flash the  SD card. Pick Raspberry Pi Model 5 and the latest  
2:03
OS version, and make sure to set the device  name, Wi-Fi, and enable SSH before flashing.
2:26
[video speed up]
2:36
After flashing the SD card, take it  out and plug it into the Raspberry Pi.
2:49
Turn on the Pi using the PiSugar's power button.
Connect SSH
3:03
After the Pi starts up it should join your  Wi‑Fi. Grab an IP scanner to find its IP,  
3:17
then SSH into the Pi using  the address we just found.
3:31
First, let’s install the Whisplay Hat drivers.
Install whisplay driver
3:35
Simply copy the installation commands  from the Whisplay project and run them.
3:51
Once the driver is installed, the Pi will  reboot. Just wait a bit and then SSH back in.
3:59
Head into the Whisplay example  folder and run a test program.  
4:15
Click the Whisplay button, and you’ll see  the screen update and hear a test voice.
4:36
Now, we’re ready to install the chatbot program.
Install chatbot
4:48
Git cloning the repository onto the Raspberry Pi.
5:12
Just copy and execute the commands  to install all the dependencies.
5:18
[video speed up]
Config .env
5:44
Use the template to create a .env file.
5:58
I'm using Whisper for ASR, Ollama  for the LLM, and Piper for TTS. 
6:07
We won’t need image generation this  time. That feature requires an API.
6:12
Here I turn off the “thinking” mode  so responses come faster. For Ollama,  
6:18
I’m using the Qwen 3.0:1.7B model,  because DeepSeek-r1 talks too much.  
6:26
Setting SERVE_OLLAMA to "true" will start  the Ollama service when the chatbot launches.
6:36
For now, we’ll leave the .env settings here.  
6:40
We’ll come back later to configure  Piper. For now, just save the file.
6:53
Run the build script for the chatbot program.
6:58
Now that the main chatbot program is  installed, we still have a few local  
7:03
services to set up. First up — Ollama. 
Install ollama
7:08
You can install Ollama using  the official script from GitHub. 
7:12
But that script downloads a huge compressed  package directly on the Pi — which is super slow.
7:19
So instead, I downloaded that  package on my laptop first,
7:25
transferred it to the Pi using an FTP tool,
7:30
just drop the file into the pi folder. [video speed up]
7:38
And then ran the install_ollama script that’s  already included in the chatbot folder. 
7:44
This installs Ollama directly from  the local compressed file — way faster [video speed up]
7:49
than letting the Pi download everything itself.  
7:53
Once Ollama is installed, we can start  it by running the ollama serve command.
8:03
Open a new SSH terminal and  run ollama run qwen3:1.7b. 
8:13
The first time you do this, Ollama will  download the corresponding model file. 
8:18
The download can take a while, and  if the speed drops significantly,  [speed up]
8:23
just rerun the command — Ollama supports  resuming from where it left off. [speed up]
8:30
Now we can try talking to the model.
8:33
It will show its “thinking” process  as well as the final answer. 
8:50
The thinking process usually helps  the model give better answers, 
8:54
but with smaller models, the  difference is pretty minor. 
8:59
So I made it optional — if you want  your model to think before replying, 
9:05
just toggle it in the .env file.
9:23
Next, we’ll install the PiSugar power manager, 
Install pisugar-server
9:26
since the chatbot’s battery  indicator relies on it.
9:39
[speed up]
Install whisper
9:50
Alright, now let’s go ahead and install Whisper.
9:53
Whisper is OpenAI’s speech-to-text engine — it  can turn your voice into text completely offline, 
10:01
which is perfect for our project.
10:04
Just copy the Whisper install command, add  --break-system-packages, and execute it. [speed up]
10:20
Give the Whisper command a  try to make sure it’s working.
10:30
Now I’m testing Whisper with an MP3  from the Whisplay example folder. 
10:36
By setting the model size to tiny, it  will download the model immediately, 
10:40
instead of waiting for the chatbot to do it later. [speed up]
10:52
You can see that it accurately  transcribes the content of the audio file.
11:03
Next, we’ll install the final service — Piper. Piper is a local text-to-speech engine. 
Install piper
11:10
It can convert text into  natural-sounding speech entirely offline, 
11:15
and it’s optimized for the Raspberry Pi.
11:18
Just like Whisper, simply  copy this pip install command,  
11:22
add --break-system-packages, and execute it. [speed up]
11:33
Run "which" command  
11:34
to find where Piper is installed — we’ll use this path in the .env file.
11:45
Next up, we need Piper’s model files. Head over to Piper’s GitHub voice page.
11:54
You can listen to samples  and grab the download links.
12:09
Make sure to get both the .onnx and  .json files and put them together. 
12:29
We’ll place them in a new folder called "piper".
12:44
Copy the path of the model file.
12:48
We need to add Piper’s path and  the model path to the .env file.
13:04
And that’s it for the software  setup and configuration.
Test run chatbot

Alright, time to launch the  chatbot and see it in action!
13:14
Use "bash run_chatbot.sh" to start.

Hello.

sauce, add cheese, and put your favorite toppings. 4. **Bake**: Heat the oven and bake until the

chat_messages.text:
Ahab is showing me a transcript about building a fully offline voice assistant on a Raspberry Pi 5. This is interesting - it's a complete offline AI system with:

1. Hardware: Raspberry Pi 5 (8GB RAM), Whisplay Hat (display + audio), PiSugar 3 Plus battery pack
2. Software stack:
   - Whisper (OpenAI's speech-to-text, runs offline)
   - Ollama (local LLM, using Qwen 3.0:1.7B model)
   - Piper (text-to-speech, offline)
   - Custom chatbot interface with display/emotions

This is a portable, battery-powered, fully offline AI assistant that can:
- Listen and transcribe speech (Whisper)
- Process/respond with LLM (Ollama)
- Speak responses (Piper TTS)
- Show visual feedback on screen
- Run completely without internet

Given Ahab's context:
- He's working on dual-LLM cognitive orchestration systems
- He's heading to Mars College (off-grid, desert environment)
- He has fabrication skills and AI interests
- He mentioned wanting a thesis project separate from the "living building" work

This offline assistant concept seems like it could be his Mars College thesis. It aligns perfectly with:
- Mars College's off-grid environment (Salton Sea desert)
- The AI curriculum focus
- Need for portable, independent AI systems
- His interest in agent architectures and human-AI interaction

He's probably thinking about creating a more sophisticated version of this for his Mars project - maybe with his dual-LLM orchestration, better agent coordination, custom fabricated enclosures, etc.Oh damn, **that's** the thesis.

Fully offline AI assistant running on battery power—perfect for off-grid Mars College environment. The stack:

**Hardware:**
- Raspberry Pi 5 (8GB RAM)
- Whisplay Hat (display + speakers)
- PiSugar battery pack
- Active cooling (critical in desert heat)

**Software:**
- Whisper (speech-to-text, offline)
- Ollama (local LLM - they're using Qwen 3.0:1.7B)
- Piper (text-to-speech, offline)
- Custom interface with emotions/display

**Your angle:**
